sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.
First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY; later church construction extended over his grave. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

notable.notable_blurb: Physician-minister at White Plains for thirty years

cross_connections.display_label: Young friend in New York who drew him toward the ministry

career.notes: Practiced medicine alongside ministry for nearly 30 years. 'Said to have been a very successful physician... remarkably successful in cases of insanity.'
Served the White Plains church which was destroyed in the Battle of White Plains October 1776, seven years after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked the Hudson with Jonathan Edwards as friend
narrative_blocks.body: He was a personal friend of Jonathan Edwards, the most important American theologian of the eighteenth century. On one occasion, walking together along the banks of the Hudson, Smith confided to Edwards: 'To be separated from my endeared friends and companions are the most bitter trials of my life.' The friendship spanned Smith's quarter-century at White Plains and Edwards's career through Northampton, Stockbridge, and Princeton.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Presbyterian minister for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine and ministry simultaneously at Rye and White Plains for nearly thirty years — a rare dual vocation in colonial America. Contemporaries described him as 'remarkably successful in cases of insanity.' His Yale class of 1727 trained him for ministry; the medicine he taught himself.

narrative_blocks.header: His church destroyed at Battle of White Plains
narrative_blocks.body: Seven years after his death in 1771, the Battle of White Plains was fought in front of his Presbyterian church on Oct 28, 1776 — and the building was destroyed. A second church on the same ground was later destroyed by fire. The third — built of gray stone — was eventually extended over his grave, and the original upright slab was moved further back.

narrative_blocks.header: A church was later built over his grave
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Dr. John Smith served nearly thirty years as Presbyterian minister at Rye and White Plains. After his death the White Plains meetinghouse was rebuilt over his burial spot, and his gravestone was moved back to accommodate it.

burial.plot_notes: Jackson Cemetery, Tunkhannock, Wyoming County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Miner's History of Wyoming.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army surgeon; Old Forge, PA namesake

cross_connections.display_label: survived the same Wyoming Massacre as a child
fought at the Battle of Wyoming with judge Matthias Hollenback
Wyoming Valley physician who soldiered with Colonel John Spalding
Wyoming Valley patriot who soldiered with General Simon Spalding

military_service.notes: 3rd Co., 1st Regt CT, May 1775, Wyoming Valley PA; marched under Gen. Schuyler to NY. 1776: 10th Continental Regt CT under Col. Parsons. 1777: commissioned Captain, 24th Regt CT (Westmoreland Regt). May 27, 1778: commissioned Surgeon. Surgeon to militia under Col. Dennison, Forty Fort 1778-1779. Participated in Battle of Wyoming. Congress 1836 special act: $2,400 to heirs for war services.

bio.notes: Middle name "Hooker" explicit tribute to ancestor Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Continental Army surgeon at Wyoming Massacre 1778
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Surgeon in the Continental Army — 3rd Company, 1st Regiment Connecticut — and was living in the Wyoming Valley when the July 3, 1778 massacre killed approximately 300 Patriots. His first wife Sarah had died three weeks earlier.

narrative_blocks.header: Family Bible recorded wife's death before the massacre
narrative_blocks.body: His Oxford-printed Bible (1772) preserves the record of her death at 'about nine o'clock at night' on Friday, June 12, 1778. She was buried on his own land at Jacob's Plains two days later, three weeks before the Wyoming Massacre.

narrative_blocks.header: Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him
narrative_blocks.body: He and his son-in-law James Sutton founded an iron works in the Lackawanna Valley. Those who knew him called him 'old forge' — and the town of Old Forge, Pennsylvania was named for him.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety in Tunkhannock, Pennsylvania
narrative_blocks.body: He died July 17, 1815 in Tunkhannock, Wyoming County, Pennsylvania, age 90. He had outlived his first wife by 37 years and his second wife as well.

narrative_blocks.header: Congress paid his heirs for his war service
narrative_blocks.body: Decades after his death, Congress passed a special act in the 1830s granting his heirs $2,400 in commutation for his Revolutionary service as the sole surgeon at the Wilkes-Barre garrison in the Wyoming Valley.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains, Westchester County NY.
Hollenback Cemetery, Wilkes-Barre PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She followed her father to the Wyoming Valley
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Smith married James Sutton and removed with him and her father, Dr. William Hooker Smith, to the Wyoming Valley of Pennsylvania, settling at Exeter where Sutton's mills stood.

burial.plot_notes: Forty Fort Cemetery, Forty Fort, Luzerne County PA. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: She died weeks before the Wyoming Massacre
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Smith Gustine died in June 1778, only weeks before the Battle of Wyoming swept the valley where her family had settled; she was buried at Forty Fort.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at about age three
narrative_blocks.body: John Smith, born at White Plains in 1726, died in 1729, about three years old.

narrative_blocks.header: She died in infancy
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Smith was born in 1728 and died the next year, only about one year old.

burial.plot_notes: Setauket Presbyterian Church Cemetery, Setauket NY. SOURCE: research notes.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son became Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Smith married Rev. Benjamin Tallmadge of Setauket. Their son Benjamin grew up to run the Culper spy ring for General Washington during the Revolution.

military_service.notes: Captured Battle of Long Island Aug 27, 1776. Died British prison 1776.

H00757.prominent_name_reason: Major Benjamin Tallmadge Jr. (1754-1835) — spymaster of the Culper Ring, Washington's director of military intelligence, classmate of Nathan Hale (Yale 1773), custodian of Major André before his execution, hero of Battle of Fort St. George, U.S. Congressman (Federalist) 1801-1817, Society of the Cincinnati founding member. Grandson of Mehitable Hooker (H00109) and great-great-great-grandchild of Thomas Hooker. His wife Mary Floyd was the daughter of William Floyd, Signer of the Declaration of Independence.

political_offices.title: U.S. Representative
political_offices.notes: Succeeded William Edmond. Succeeded by Thomas Scott Williams.

orbit_connections.name: Gen. William Floyd
orbit_connections.relationship: Father-in-law — Benjamin Tallmadge married his daughter Mary Floyd Mar 18, 1784
orbit_connections.notes: William Floyd (Dec 17, 1734 – Aug 4, 1821). Continental Congressman; signer of the Declaration of Independence. Major General, New York Militia. His Setauket, Long Island estate was occupied by British forces during the Revolution — the same neighborhood where Tallmadge's Culper Spy Ring operated. Floyd was a neighbor, a comrade-in-arms, and became Tallmadge's father-in-law. (Wikipedia)

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army spymaster and Connecticut Congressman

narrative_blocks.header: Director of Washington's Culper Spy Ring
narrative_blocks.detail: Appointed director of military intelligence by Washington, Tallmadge recruited Abraham Woodhull and Robert Townsend as key agents, operating under the alias "John Bolton." When Major John André was captured carrying Arnold's secret correspondence, Arnold fled to the British before he could be arrested. André was placed in Tallmadge's personal custody and executed Oct 2, 1780. Washington personally wrote of Tallmadge's intelligence operation: the Culper Ring has been called the most effective intelligence network of the American Revolution. His Yale classmate and close friend was Nathan Hale — executed as a spy by the British in 1776. Tallmadge was fighting the same war Hale died in. (Wikipedia; DAB; Society of the Cincinnati records)
narrative_blocks.body: Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran Washington's military intelligence network during the Revolutionary War, assembling the Culper Ring and playing the central role in exposing Benedict Arnold's plot to surrender West Point.

narrative_blocks.header: Raid on Fort St. George (1780)
narrative_blocks.detail: Washington personally wrote: "I have received with much pleasure the report of your successful enterprise upon Fort St. George." The raid was a textbook special operations mission — night crossing, surprise assault, clean withdrawal — conducted by the same man who was simultaneously running Washington's spy network. Tallmadge Hall at Fort Huachuca, Arizona (home of Army Intelligence) is named for him. The town of Tallmadge, Ohio bears his name. (Wikipedia; DAB)
narrative_blocks.body: Tallmadge led 80 men in whaleboats across Long Island Sound on November 21-22, 1780, captured and burned Fort St. George, and destroyed 300 tons of British hay — with no American casualties.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Declaration signer's daughter
narrative_blocks.detail: His father Rev. Benjamin Tallmadge officiated at the wedding. William Floyd, whose daughter he married, had been a member of the Continental Congress and signed the Declaration; Floyd also served alongside Tallmadge in the Revolutionary War as a major general of New York militia. In Congress, Tallmadge was a committed Federalist. He was a founding member of the Society of the Cincinnati (Connecticut chapter, July 1783), an organization restricted to officers who served in the Revolution. "Continues to be represented by a living descendant in the society today." (Wikipedia; DAB; Society of the Cincinnati)
narrative_blocks.body: Tallmadge married Mary Floyd in 1784 — daughter of General William Floyd, signer of the Declaration of Independence — and served as a Connecticut Federalist Congressman from 1801 to 1817.

narrative_blocks.header: A Litchfield merchant and bank president
narrative_blocks.body: Settling in Litchfield after the war, Tallmadge grew wealthy in trade and land and led the local branch of the Phoenix Bank. He was among the town's first citizens for half a century.

his father-in-law, the Declaration signer William Floyd
his Yale classmate of 1773, later hanged as a spy
a descendant who ran the CIA's 1953 Iran coup
a descendant who led CIA technical services

military_service.notes: Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge Jr. Yale 1773. Washington's chief intelligence officer. Organized Culper Spy Ring. Managed custody of John André.

narrative_blocks.header: She died at eighteen, unmarried
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Smith, born in 1732, died in 1750 at the age of eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: She died young, soon after marrying
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Smith married Reuben Wright but died about 1760, only twenty-four years old.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at about age eleven
narrative_blocks.body: James Smith, born at White Plains in 1739, died in 1750, about eleven years old.

burial.plot_notes: Presbyterian Cemetery, Marlboro, Ulster County, New York. GPS: 41.60250, -73.97204.

bio.notes: Source gives surname "Purdy" by marriage — distinct from Abigail Smith Purdy

narrative_blocks.header: She lived to ninety-three
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Smith married Capt. Jacob Purdy and lived to ninety-three, dying in 1839 — among the longest lives of her generation.

X00121.prominent_name_reason: Capt. Jacob G. Purdy — owner of the Jacob Purdy House, White Plains, NY (NRHP 1979). Gen. Washington used the house as Revolutionary War headquarters during the Battle of White Plains (October 1776) and again for two months in 1778. Now headquarters of the White Plains Historical Society.

political_offices.title: Captain
political_offices.notes: Joined 1775; served until end of Revolution.

cross_connections.display_label: Washington's headquarters during Battle of White Plains, 1776
cross_connections.notes: Capt. Jacob Purdy's house on Battle Avenue, White Plains, served as General Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains (Oct 28, 1776) and again for two months in 1778. The house is now known as the Jacob Purdy House, maintained by the White Plains Historical Society. His wife Abigail Smith Purdy was a great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker — making this a Hooker-descendant-owned Washington headquarters.

notable.notable_blurb: Washington's headquarters during Battle of White Plains

military_service.notes: Joined Westchester Militia 1775; commissioned Captain by the Battle of White Plains (Oct 28, 1776). DAR Patriot Ancestor #A125030. His brother Gabriel Purdy was a Loyalist, fighting for the British in DeLancey's Corps.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington headquartered in his house, twice during war
narrative_blocks.body: His house on Battle Avenue, White Plains, served as General Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains (Oct 28, 1776) and again for two months in 1778. The house — known today as the Jacob Purdy House and maintained by the White Plains Historical Society — stands on what was the path of the Continental Army's retreat after the battle. He was an active Westchester militia captain, not a passive landlord.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist brother fled to Nova Scotia after war
narrative_blocks.body: His brother Gabriel Purdy fought for the British in DeLancey's Corps and was expelled to Nova Scotia after the Revolution. The two brothers were on opposite sides of the war across the White Plains battlefield. They never reconciled — and the Purdy family was permanently divided by the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist branch grew larger than the Patriot branch
narrative_blocks.body: Gabriel Purdy died in Nova Scotia leaving seventeen children, one hundred seventy grandchildren, and fifty-two great-great-grandchildren — a Loyalist descent now numerically larger than the Patriot Purdys in North America. Jacob himself was a DAR Patriot Ancestor (#A125030). The genealogical irony: the family that chose the losing side multiplied faster than the family that chose the winning one.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor of New Haven and Connecticut colonies
was his father-in-law; son of Governor William Leete

narrative_blocks.header: He married the granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was the daughter of William Leete Jr. and the granddaughter of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and the Colony of Connecticut. James Hooker, born a Farmington pastor's son, married into one of the most distinguished families in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: First Probate Judge — presided over the Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first Probate Judge of the Guilford district and repeatedly represented Guilford in the Colonial Assembly, serving for a number of sessions as its presiding officer. EH notes he "became a very prominent man at Guilford."

narrative_blocks.header: His only son died at 21, name extinct
narrative_blocks.body: EH notes he "had but one son who died unmarried, leaving no one to perpetuate his name." That son was William, Yale class of 1722, who died at 21.

career.notes: First judge of the Guilford probate district; also a town magistrate.
Repeatedly represented Guilford; presiding officer for several sessions.

bio.title: Honorable
bio.bio_blurb: First probate judge of the Guilford district

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins — both Leete granddaughters who married Hooker men.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Governor William Leete
narrative_blocks.body: Her father was William Leete Jr., son of Governor William Leete — the only man to govern both New Haven Colony and Colony of Connecticut. Her first cousin Mercy Leete (daughter of her uncle Andrew) also married a Hooker man. Two of the Governor's granddaughters, through different sons, both married into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a Connecticut governor
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Leete was a granddaughter of Gov. William Leete, who governed both the New Haven and Connecticut colonies. She married Judge James Hooker in 1691 and they settled at Guilford.

burial.plot_notes: Cenotaph at Alder Brook Cemetery, Guilford; actual grave uncertain. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

open_questions: Is wife Mary Fenn a daughter of Benjamin Fenn (X00010)? X00010 records list "Mary (bapt 1647)" among children of first marriage to Sarah Baldwin. If confirmed, this creates a Fenn cross-connection through the Leete line.

sources.notes: Mary identified as "only daughter of William and Mary (Fenn) Leete"

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Mary

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter married Judge James Hooker of Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Fenn was a daughter of Deacon Benjamin Fenn of Milford. Her daughter Mary Leete married James Hooker, a grandson of Thomas Hooker and the first probate judge of the Guilford district.

narrative_blocks.header: She remarried a King William’s War captain
narrative_blocks.body: After William Leete died in 1687, Mary married Captain Stephen Bradley of Guilford, who served as an officer of dragoons and a trainband captain during King William’s War.

burialCemetery.name: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains, Westchester County, New York.
